text: ANTULLY, a commune and village of France, dep. of Saone-et-Loire, arrond. of Autun; 49 m. NW of Macon. Pop. 1,432.
ANTUN, a river in the island of Banca. At about 5 m. from its mouth is a village of the same name, whence great quantities of tin are exported.
ANTUN ELIAS, a town of Syria, in the pash. of Acre, on the coast of the Mediterranean, between the Nahr-el-Kelb and Beirut, in lat. 33° 52' N.

ANTURAH, or ANTURA, a village of Syria, in the pash. of Acre, 15 m. NE of Beirut; 36 m. S of Tripoli. It is delightfully situated on Libanus, and surrounded by plantations of mulberry trees. The Lazzarists have a college here with about 35 students, principally Europeans.

ANTURLI, a town and district of Hindostan, in the prov. of Candeish, on the Tuptee, 10 m. SW of Burhanpur. In 1820, it was divided between Sindia and the British government as successors to the Peishwa. ANTWERP, [FLEM. Antwerpen, FR. Anvers,] a prov. of Belgium, situated between lat. 51° and 51° 30' N, and long. 4° 10' and 5° 10' E; bounded on the N and NE by the Dutch prov. of N Brabant; on the SE by the prov. of Limburg; on the S by S Brabant; on the W it is separated from E Flanders by the Scheldt. Its greatest breadth from N to S is 30 m.; and its length from NE to SW, 60 m. Its superficial area is 283,311 hectares. The principal rivers are the Scheldt, which near the city of Antwerp is 2,160 ft. broad and 30 deep; the Great and the Little Nethe which unite and form the Ruppel; the Dyle, and the Senne; of minor importance are the Aa, the Byloop, the Marck, the Schyn, and the Vliet. The prov. is likewise intersected by four canals, one from Lierre to Herenthals, formed by a series of works on the Little Nethe; another from Turnhout to the Maestricht canal in Limburg; the third is the canal of Louvain; and the fourth that of Willebroeck.-The surface of the prov. is a perfect level; and so low that water may always be found at a depth of 8 or 10 inches. The soil generally consists of a light sand, resting on a substratum of humid clay, and covered with a fertile vegetable mould. In the N and E districts of the province are extensive moor lands, presenting numerous lakes and morasses, and in some parts sterile tracts of white sand. The best agricultural soil is in the neighbourhood of Mechlin. The beautiful meadows of the Scheldt, the Ruppel, and the two Nethes, owe their preservation to the constant care which is bestowed upon them. Notwithstanding the disadvantages of its soil, the industry of the inhabitants derives from it a greater supply of corn than is required for the consumption of the prov. It produces likewise madder, hops, tobacco, rape-seed, and lint; the little wood it possesses consists chiefly of fir. In the S districts the roads, which are generally well made, are bordered with trees. The cattle are good; the rivers abound with fish; and the rich pastures afford delicious honey.—The principal manufactures are lace, silk, printed calicoes, linen and cotton fabrics, straw-hats, hosiery, wax-cloth, tobacco, and salt. Sugar-refineries, distilleries of vinegar, breweries, soap-works, and tanneries, are numerous.—The commerce of the prov. is still considerable, although greatly inferior to what it was in the 15th and 16th cent. The proximity of the sea renders the climate more mild than might be expected from its northern situation; but it is very damp and foggy, and the temperature is very variable.—This prov. is divided into the 3 arrondissements of Antwerp, Mechlin, and Turnhout; which are subdivided into 16 cantons; and these again into the 4 town-communes of Antwerp, Mechlin, Lierre, and Turnhout, and 141 ruralcommunes. The pop. in 1846 amounted to 406,358; in 1829 it was 343,214.—It sends to the legislative chambers 3 senators and 10 representatives.—The province of A. constitutes the bishopric of Mechlin. It was inhabited by the Ambivarites before Cæsar's conquest. By the Romans it was comprised in Germania Secunda. As part of Austrasia, it was separated from Neustria by the Scheldt. At a later period it was divided into the Campine which comprised the NE portion of the prov., and the Reyen which lay along the r. bank of the Scheldt. As a part of the Lotheringian kingdom, it was incorporated with Lower Lorraine. The marquisate of Antwerp first appears in history in the year 1080; and, with the lordship of Mechlin, seems to have comprised nearly the whole of the present prov. of A. In 1795, on its being incorporated with the French republic, it formed the dep. of Deux Nèthes.

ANTWERP, the cap. of the above prov., occupies a low situation on the r. bank of the Scheldt, in N lat. 51° 13′ 14" and E long. 4° 24′, 75 m. S of Amsterdam, 25 m. due N of Brussels or 281 m. by railway, 9 m. above Fort Lillo on the Scheldt, and 45 m. above Flushing or the mouth of the river. In the days of Guicciardini, this city contained 200 streets, 22 magnificent squares, and 13,500 houses. It now contains 11 canals, which penetrate to the interior of the town, and connect its ports with Mechlin, Louvain, and Brussels; 162 streets, well-paved, broad and straight; 22 public squares, and 10,088 houses, well-built of brick or stone. In the Place de Meer is the imperial palace of Napoleon. The town-house is a handsome building; and the exchange is considered one of the finest in Europe. A. has five parishchurches, less remarkable for their architecture than for the works of art of which they are the depositaries; but the principal object of interest in A. is its cathedral, which is one of the finest specimens of Gothic architecture in Europe. It has sixty-six chapels; and the paintings above the altars are by Rubens, citadel, the arsenal on the N bank of the Scheldt-here a river much larger than the Thames at London—the great wet-docks, the district towns, and far in the west the shadowy islands with which the mouth of the Scheldt is crowded. For two-thirds of its height it is square and solid; but it then becomes hexagonal and more elaborate; towards the top it is nearly all fretwork. The church is 500 ft. long, 230 wide, and 360 high. It has, from time to time, suffered considerable injury from fire and revolutionary violence. The most serious mischief occurred in 1797, when several gold and silver vessels, and other highlyprized treasures, were carried off or destroyed. In 1810 important repairs and improvements took place; and, in 1816, some of the most valuable paintings of the Flemish school, which had been removed to France, were replaced. On entering by the west door, the vast expanse is strikingly grand. In the centre of the cathedral is the lofty dome, from the sides of which light is admitted, displaying a richlycoloured picture of the Virgin, surmounted by a choir of angels. Nearer to the choir, and on the wall facing the west, are two magnificent pictures by Rubens. The most celebrated of these productions, 'The Descent from the Cross,' is generally considered his chief work. The two wings-which are pictures attached to it by hinges, and are much narrower though of the same height-are by the same great artist, and represent 'The Purification,' and 'The Visitation.' The companion to 'The Descent,' on the left is 'The Elevation of the Cross.' The museum contains 127 chief-d'œuvres of the Flemish school. A. likewise possesses a royal academy of arts constituted in 1817, a public library, a botanic garden, a medical school, a general hospital, several alms-houses, and a great military arsenal. The city is environed with a large wall, planted with rows of trees on each side, with walks between broad enough for two coaches to go abreast; and it is also defended by a large, strong, and regular citadel, erected by the duke of Alva in 1568. It is a regular pentagon in shape, protected by bastions ranging at progressive elevations, and connecting themselves with curtains of proportionate height. In advance of these defences is a further series of spacious bastions erected in 1701. The ditch is 90 ft. wide, and has 18 ft. of water in every part of its circuit, supplied from the Scheldt by means of two small sluices and a subterranean passage. The western banks of the Scheldt can also be put under water. An esplanade of the average width of 400 paces, which was laid out as a promenade before the recent bombardment, separates the citadel from the town. This celebrated citadel has sustained sundry blockades and sieges in 1576, 1583, 1585, 1706, 1748, 1789, 1792, 1793, 1814, and 1832. No expense was spared by Bonaparte to deepen the river, enlarge the harbour, and strengthen the fortifications of A., which he intended to make one of his strong naval stations. The area of the great basin is 17:16 English acres; and of the small one, 7 acres. On each side of the great basin are two careening docks for repairing ships-of-war. The commerce of A. is still considerable, though far below what it was in the 15th and 16th cent. According to Dutch authorities, only 995 ships entered its port in 1829; and 690 in 1830. We have before us a general statistical return from the prov. of A. in 1837, which gives the following comparative view of the commerce and navigation of this port during the three years mentioned:- Foreign. Total. Tonnage. Belgian. Thus it appears that there was, in 1837, an increase of 48,951 tons above 1836; and of 72,787 tons above 1835. This increase was chiefly in arrivals from the North with corn and timber. The exports consisted of raw produce of different kinds, manufactured articles, and refined sugar. In 1840 the arrivals with cargoes were 1,151 ships, of a total tonnage of 176,435 tons. The port of A., in 1836, possessed 61 ships of all classes and dimensions, measuring in all 10,010 tons, including two steam-boats, which measured together 1,222 tons. In 1840 it possessed 60 sailing vessels, measuring in all 9,611 tons; and 4 steamers. As to its inland trade, A. is connected by canals with the towns of Mechlin, Louvain, Brussels, and with Ghent by the Scheldt. There is a railway from A. to Ghent, by Beveren, St. Nicholas, Lokeren, and Beirvelde, 32 m. in length, which connects it with Bruges also, and Ostend; while on the other side it is connected by railway with Liege, 71 m.; Aix-la-Chapelle, 1-16 m.; and Cologne, 150 m. The railway to Quievrain, on the French frontier, near Valenciennes, by Malines, Brussels, and Mons, is 130 kilometres or 64 miles in length. — The chief manufactures are thread, tape, linen, cloth, silks, sugar-refining, calico-printing, and diamond-cutting. A. exports flax and bark to Great Britain; and madder, refined sugar, and Belgian manufactured articles to other places. The French coinage is current, and also the French weights and measures. The language which is most in use among the higher classes is the French; Dutch is also much spoken; but the Flemish is the true language of the country and of the majority of the people. The shopkeepers, and all classes except the lowest, are able to speak French; and most of the newspapers are published in that language. This celebrated city boasts of great antiquity; it has been supposed to be the capital of the Abivariti of Cæsar. It was ravaged by the Normans in 836 or 837, but had again risen to importance in 1124. It attained the acme of its splendour and commercial prosperity about the middle of the 16th cent. Its fortifications and principal buildings are attributed to that epoch. In the zenith of its commercial splendour, in 1500, it contained more than 200,000 inhabitants. The conduct of the Dutch towards A. affords one of the most striking instances of commercial jealousy recorded in the page of history. Though A. had taken part in the cause of liberty equally with themselves, and had consequently been subjected to the loss of its commerce and the depopulation of its inhabitants, yet when besieged for more than a year by the Duke of Parma, in 1584-5, the Dutch merchants of Amsterdam used secret means to prevent assistance being given their rival brethren. At the peace of Munster, in which Spain acknowledged the Dutch independence, A. was sacrificed to Dutch interests; for it was agreed by that treaty that no large vessel should sail up to A. without having unloaded her cargo in a Dutch port, whence the merchandise might be conveyed in barges or small craft. The emperor Joseph attempted to open the navigation of the Scheldt, but was prevented by the resolute opposition of the Dutch, supported by the king of Prussia. But when the French obtained possession of A., in August 1794, they immediately declared it a free port, opened up the navigation of the river, and dismantled all those forts which the Dutch had constructed at vast expense to prevent vessels from going up to Antwerp. The last memorable event in the history of A. is the capture of its citadel by the French under Marshal Gerard. The king of Holland having refused to evacuate the citadel of A., conformably to the terms agreed on by the high contracting powers who arranged the separation of Holland and Belgium, the French entered Belgium in November 1832, with about 66,000 men. The citadel of A. was defended by General Chassé, for the king of Holland, with 4,500 men. The French broke ground on the 29th of November; on the 14th of December they made a breach in the face of Fort St. Laurent by three mines, and immediately took the fort by assault. They then directed their breaching-battery against the Toledo bastion; and on the 24th the citadel surrendered. The only result of the obstinacy of the king of Holland was the loss of much life, and the infliction of much human suffering. The loss of the French, according to official reports, was 108 killed, and 695 wounded. The loss of the Dutch, in killed, wounded, and missing, was between 500 and 600. Since the consolidation of the Belgian provinces under the sovereignty of their present monarch, the tide of prosperity which formerly filled its port with the treasures of the world, has again set in towards A., and promises to render it once more one of the great commercial cities of Europe.—A. was the birth-place of the painters Teniers, Vandyke, and Jordaens, and of the geographer Ortelius, and the celebrated printer C. Plantyn.
ANTWERP, a township of New York, U. S., in Jefferson co., 20 m. NE of Watertown, and 169 NW of Albany. It is watered by the Oswegatchie river on the N, and the Indian on the S. Pop. 3,109.
ANUNE, a town of New Granada, in the prov. and about 70 m. ENE of Caguan; in N lat. 3° 16'.
ANVE'VILLE, a commune of France, in the dep. of La Seine Inférieure, arrond. of Yvetot, and cant. of Ourville. Pop. 1,011.
ANVILLE, a township of Pennsylvania, U. S., in Lebanon co. Pop. in 1840, 2,949.

ANWEILER, a small town of Bavaria, in the district of Landau, on the r. bank of the Queich, 7 m. W of Landau. It has several tanneries, paper-mills, and distilleries of kirschwasser. The castle, built by the emperor Frederick, and in which for a long period the jewels of the Crown were kept, is now in ruins. A. was formerly a free town of the empire.
ANWICK, a parish in Lincolnshire, 5 m. ENE of Sleaford. Area 1,820 acres. Pop. 314.
ANWORTH, a parish in Kirkcudbright, SW of Gatehouse, from which it is separated by the Fleet. Pop. 883.
ANXIETY (POINT), a headland on the coast of Russian America, in N lat. 70° 10′, W long. 147° 30′.

ANXIOUS BAY, an indentation on the W coast of Eyre Land, in Southern Australia, in Slat. 33° 45'. ANY, ANEY, or KOCKANEY, a parish in co. Limerick, 2 m. SW by S of Hospital. Area 9,248 acres. Pop. 407.
ANY-MARTIN-RIEUX, a commune and village ~ ANZ of France, in the dep. of Aisne, and cant. of Auben ton. Pop. 1,066.

ANZA, a river of Piedmont, which rises on the W side of Mont Rosa, and after a course of about 17 m. through the Val d'Anzasca, on the frontier of the Valais, celebrated for its gold mines, unites with the Toce nearly opposite Vogogna.
ANZANEGO, a town of Spain in Arragon, prov. of Huesca, on the l. bank of the Gallego, 25 m. NNW of Huesco.

ANZANO, a village of Naples, in the prov. of Capitanata, and cant. of Accadia. Pop. 800. It is prettily situated on a rising ground 7 m. SSW of Bovino; and is supposed to occupy the site of the Trevice of Horace.
ANZAT-LE-LUGET, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dome, cant. of Ardes. Pop. 1,817. There are some mines of antimony here.
ANZELY. See INZELY.
ANZEME, a commune of France, in the dep. of La Creuse, cant. of St. Vawry. Pop. 1,514.

ANZERSKOI, an island of Russia, in the White sea, 147 m. WSW of Archangel, and 66 m. ENE of Kem. It is said to contain mines of silver and copper. ANZEYRYS, ANSAIRIANS, or NASSAIRIANS, a wild tribe of Arabs, who live secluded in their mountain-fastnesses to the N of Lebanon. Their numbers have been estimated at 60,000. On the lower hills of the A. country the governor of Latakia exercises feeble authority; on the upper ranges, the A. are governed by their own hereditary beys and sheikhs. Maundrell calls them Neceres; Pococke, Nocires; Niebuhr, Nassariens; D'Anville, Nassaris; Delisle, Ensyriens; Volney, Ansarié and Ansariens. We have adopted Burckhardt's orthography in Anzeyrys.
ANZI, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Basilicata, 8 m. SE of Potenza. Pop. 3,200.

ANZICO, an extensive region in the interior of Western Africa, to the N of Congo. Very little is known relative to this district; the people are said to be brave and active, but ferocious in their habits. Their capital is called Monsol.

ANZIN, a commune and town of France, in the dep. du Nord, arrond. of Douay, 1 m. NW of Valenciennes, in the centre of one of the most important coal-fields in France. Pop. 4,255. It is a prolongation of the Dutch coal-field. It embraces an extent of 500 sq. m., and varies in depth from 140 to 1,640 ft. Its produce of coal in 1846 amounted to 5,831,756 quintals, valued at 7,506,499 francs, which is raised and distributed by one colossal establishment, which keeps in regular employment 80 clerks, 1,900 pit-men, and 209 horses. The excellent system of management which is maintained in the collieries has greatly contributed to their success. Coal is conveyed from this coal-field to Paris by means of the canal of St. Quentin; and it has given existence to the numerous and varied manufactures scattered over the industrious Dep. du Nord. At Anzin there are glasshouses, blast - furnaces, iron - forges, salt-refineries, breweries, distilleries, brick-kilns, and nail-works.
ANZO (Porto D'), a seaport in the Campagna di Roma, 30 m. SE of Rome, on the site of the ancient Antium. Pop. 300.

ANZORGETTI, or Oustourgeti, the capital of Guriel, in Imeritia, 76 m. SW of Cotatis, in N lat. 41° 45′, E long. 75° 10′. ANZUAN, HINZUAN, or JOHANNA, one of the Comoro group of islands, in the Mozambique channel. It is about 75 m. in circumf. Its shores are bold, and its interior is mountainous. Its highest summits attain an elevation of 6,000 ft. Its extreme southern point lies in S lat. 12° 25'; its most eastern, in E long. 44° 34'. The principal productions are cocoa-nuts, limes, oranges, yams, rice, cattle, and poultry. The pop. is supposed to amount to 100,000. The natives are below the middle size, and slightly formed; their complexion is lighter than the Mulatto. Arabic is the language spoken. - The principal town, called Matsamudo, lies on the coast; but the sultan resides in another town, called Domoni, in the interior.
ANZUELOS, a river of Costa Rica, in Guatemala, flowing into the Caribbean sea in N lat. 10° 5'.
ANZUGUIAMA, a town of Japan, in the island of Nifon, 80 m. NE of Miaco.
ANZUOLA, a town of Spain, in Guipuscoa, 8 m. W of Villafranca. Pop. 1,000.
AOIZ, a town of Spain, in Navarre, on the Iraci, 13 m. NE of Pampeluna. Pop. 1,000.

AOR (Pulo), a small island on the E coast of Malacca, in N lat. 2° 30', E long. 104° 34'. It is the point of departure for ships bound to Canton, and for which vessels generally steer on the homeward passage. It is high and covered with trees. A bay on the NW side affords good shelter and anchorage during the NE monsoon.

AORAI, a lofty mountain in the island of Tahiti. Its summit commands a magnificent view, bounded to the S by the peaks of Orohena and Pitohiti; on the E and W by the rapid succession of ridge and gorge which characterises Tahitian scenery; and on the N, overlooking a plain studded with groves of cocoa-nut and orange, and the harbour and its encircling reefs. Beyond the height at which the wild banana grows, the sides of this mountain are chiefly covered with a wiry grass, which in some places attains a height of 10 ft. It was ascended by the officers of the Peacock, in Captain Wilkes' exploring expedition.

AOSTA, a prov. of Piedmont, which has the title of a duchy; bounded on the N by Switzerland; on the E by Novarre; on the S by Turin; and on the W separated from Savoy by the Alps. It is enclosed by mountains on its northern and southern sides; while at its western extremity, shooting up its white summit far above the clouds, stands the enormous mass of Mont Blanc. It consists chiefly of one long valley, lying along the course of the Dora-Baltea or Doire which descends from the Graian Alps, in a course from NW to SE. The lower grounds are fertile in barley, oats, rye, and fruit; and the mountains abound in copper and iron. The length of this prov. from E to W is above 55 m.; it breadth 30 m. Pop. in 1826, 64,640; in 1839, 78,110. Brockden says the peasantry are squalid and filthy to an extreme degree, and that 1 in 50 is a crétin. The goitre is common amongst them. Italian is the language of the upper classes; the vernacular dialect is a French patois resembling that of Western Switzerland.—The city of A., the capital of the district, and a bishop's see subordinate to the bishop of Milan, is seated at the foot of the Alps, on the r. bank of the Dora, where the roads from the Valais and Savoy to Piedmont meet; in N lat. 45° 44' 10", E long. 7° 20'. Alt. above sea-level 1,940 ft. It is 25 m. NW of Ivrea, and 50 m. NNW of Turin. Pop. 7,000. The average annual temperature at A. is a little above 11° centigrade.—Also a town near the coast of Syria, 35 m. S of Tripoli. A Maronite patriarch resides here.
AOSTE, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Isère, cant. of Pont-de-Beauvoisin. Pop. 1,150.

AOTEA, or GREAT BARRIER, an island of New Zealand, at the entrance of the gulf of Hauraki. It is nearly 80 m. in circumf., contains much kauri forest, and has an excellent harbour at its NW extremity.
AOUZ, a town near the Red sea, in Arabia Petræa, 95 m. S of Kalaat-el-Moilah.
AOWIN, a territory on the Gold coast of Africa, E of the Assini river. The capital is Tagua.
AOYCA, a town of Spain, in Navarre, 10 m. NW of Pampeluna.
APACHERIAS, a tribe of native Indians residing in the interior of Mexico, about N lat. 34°, W long. 110°. APA

APACHES, a race of American Indians divided into several tribes, and residing between 30° and 34° N lat., from the Rio Colorado on the west, to the Rio Colorado de Texas on the east. The principal tribes are the A.-Chiricaguis, the A.-Faraones, the A:- Gilenos, the A.-Mescaleros, the A.-Mimbrenos, and the A.-Tontos. This brave nation waged perpetual war with the Spaniards during their occupation of the country.
APACZA, a town of Transylvania, on the Aluta river, 28 m. N of Cronstadt.
APAE, one of the New Hebrides group, in the Pacific ocean, in S lat. 16°, E long. 168° 30'.
APAFALVA, a village in Transylvania, in the co. of Borsode, 12 m. N of Erlan. It gave title to the illustrious family of Apafi, which became extinct on the demise of Prince Michael II., in 1713.
APAKUKIT, a town in the peninsula of Malacca, 6 m. SE of Allestar. The inhabitants are Chulias, or natives of the coast of India.

APALACHES, or St. Mark's RIVER, a river of the United States, which rises in East Florida, in N lat. 31° 30', and after a course of 135 m., falls into Apalachee bay, 15 m. below St. Mark's, near the mouth of the Apalachicola.
APALACHIAN MOUNTAINS. See ALLEGHANY MOUNTAINS.

APALACHICOLA, a port-of-entry, the cap. of Franklin co., in Florida, 135 m. SW of Tallahassee, on a bluff at the mouth of a river of the same name. Pop. 5,000. It supplies provisions to the interior, and receives cotton from it chiefly for export to England.—The A. river is formed by the Chattahoochee and Flint rivers, which unite in N lat. 30° 42', near the northern border of Florida. The A., after a course of about 70 m., flows into St. George's sound in the gulf of Mexico, and is navigable throughout for schooners of considerable size. The Chattahoochee, the western and largest tributary of the A., rises in the Apalachian or Alleghany mountains, on the confines of Georgia and Tenessee, and is navigable for boats nearly 400 m. from the gulf of Mexico. The whole country drained by the A. and its branches, is about 20,000 sq. m.
APAM, the chief seaport of Acron, on the Gold coast of Africa, 50 m. ENE of Cape coast, in N lat. 5° 15'. The Dutch have a fort here. The town was destroyed in 1811 by the Ashantees.

APANAMA, an island in the Pacific ocean, in N lat. 27° 21′, E long. 173° 57½′. It is 10 m. in length, lying NW and SE, and 5 m. in breadth. It is the Hopper island of Duperrey, and Simpson's island of Arrowsmith.— Wilkes.
APANECA, a town of Guatemala, in the prov. of Sonsonate, between the town of that name and Aguachapa, at the foot of a mountain of the same name. Pop. 1,000.
APANO-ACHAIA, a town of the Morea, on a branch of the Camenitza, 15 m. SSW of Patras.
APANOKRE'PA, or MOUNT KHREPA, the highest summit of Mount Mænalium on the Morea, between Tripolitza and Mantineia.

APANORMIA, a town on the NW coast of the island of Santorin, at the mouth of the Archipelago. It has a spacious harbour in the form of a half-moon; but the bottom is too deep for small vessels to anchor in. It is in N lat. 36° 38', E long. 25° 25'.
APARI, a town on the N coast of Luzon, one of the Philippines, in N lat. 18° 36'.
APASCO, a small town of Mexico, between Queretaro and Calaya, near the Laxa, a branch of the Santiago.

APASSI, a river of Circassia, joining the Kuban, 75 m. E of Taman. APASTEPEC. a village of Guatemala, about a league from San Vincente, noted for its annual fair on the 1st of November, for the sale of dyeing woods, &c, ANDERS FOR FOR ANDERS SO IN AND AND AND AND AND AND AND AND AND AN
APATIN, a town of Hungary, co. of Bacs, on the 1. bank of the Danube, 10 m. SW of Zombor. Pop. about 5,100.
APATSHINSK, a Russian settlement in Kamtschatka, on the Bolschaia, 25 m. from Bolscheretsk.

APCHE'RON, or Abcheron, a district of Georgia, in the possession of Russia, forming a peninsula which advances into the Caspian sea, and marks the eastern extremity of the Caucasian range, in N lat. 40° 21'. Its heights and cliffs are composed of chalky rocks, upon which repose scattered masses of brown freestone. It is remarkable for its fountains of proto-carburated hydrogen gas, -see Ateschen; its naphtha pits,—see BAKAHANY; and its salt lakes. These last are 10 in number, and the annual quantity of salt produced by them is about 150,000 poods.- Its chief town is Baku.
APCHON, a commune of France, in the dep. of Cantal, cant. of Riom-es-Montagne. Pop. 952.
APE, a town and castle of the duchy of Oldenburg, in the district of Neuenburg.
APE ISLE, an island on the coast of Algiers, off the western side of Ape's hill or Mount Abyla, in N lat. 36° 59', E long. 6° 52'.
APELBO, a town of Dalecarlia, in Sweden, in N lat. 60° 28', on the l. bank of the Dal-elf.
APELCHE, a town of Holland, in West Friesland, in N lat. 52° 55', E long. 6° 15'.
APELDOORN, a town of Holland, in Gueldreland, 10 m. NW of Zutphen. Pop. 2,670.
APENBURG, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of Saxony, 10 m. SE of Salzwedel. Pop. 470.

THE APENNINES. An uninterrupted chain of mountains in Italy, which, detaching themselves from the Low Maritime Alps, at Mount Cassino, a little to the east of the Col de Tende, and running at first in a semicircle round the gulf of Genoa, then traversing Central Italy, and finally, Southern Italy to the strait of Messina, extend through the whole Italian peninsula. The extent of the very sinuous line described by this mountain-ridge, is 840 geographical miles; its bearing, or line of course, lies between 38° and 44° 34' N lat., and 7° 40' and 18° 20' E long. of Greenwich. The elevation of the A. is much inferior to that of the Alps, as their ridge generally does not exceed 4,200 ft. of mean height; many of its summits are below 6,400 ft.; and the highest summit, the Gran Sasso in Monte Corno, in Naples, 65 m. NE by E of Rome, and near to Aquila, does not exceed 9,542 ft., according to Schouw. The A. range sends off numerous branches towards the Po, the Adriatic, and the Medi- These branches are chiefly spurs, or terranean. ranges of small hills, resting directly on the main chain; only a few form partial systems. The A. may be divided into six portions, which take their names from their relative geographical position, namely, the Northern A., the Central A., the Sub-Tuscan A., the Sub-Roman A., the Southern A., and the Sub-Vesuvian A. The Northern Apennines.] These commence at the sources of the Tanaro and Roya, at Mount Cassino; the former stream running north to the Po, and the latter to the Mediterranean. They consist of three divisions.—The first constitutes the dividingline in the western part of the Riviere de Genoa, between the streams which run N to the Po, and those that go S to the Mediterranean. This part of the chain extends from Mount Cassino, to the Bocchetta, a pass above the sources of the Lemme, and 72 geographical miles from the point of commencement. Its direction is first S, which it changes the coast; being at its western extremity 15 m., and at the Bocchetta 6 m. from the sea. Many passes exist in this first part between Piedmont and the western part of the coast of Genoa; the principal are those by the vallies of the Tanaro, the Bormida, and the Lemme, which lead to Oneglia, Savona, and Genoa. To the S, the mountains present a very steep face; but towards the north they prolong themselves beyond the Tanaro, even to the Po, forming, between Turin and Alexandria, a very mountainous region, which separates the plain of the Lower Stura, from that where the Bormida and Scrivia pursue their winding courses to the Po. The summit of the Bocchetta is the highest point of this first portion, being 6,660 feet above the sea, according to Schouw. — The second division stretches from the Bocchetta to the summit below which rises the Reno. Its extent is 108 geographical miles. Running first E and then SE, it separates the duchies of Parma and Modena from the principalities of Massa, Carrara, and Lucca. Its highest summits-among which are the Sopotorlo, the Gottro, and the Jorame, which give birth to the Trebbia, the Taro, and the Secchia, affluents of the Po-crown the upper angle of the sloping plains, which, towards the sea, become extended proportionally to their distance from Genoa; the coast of the Riviere de Levant bending towards the S. The Tidone, the Trebbia, the Nura, the Taro, and the Panaro, are the principal torrents and rivers which belong to the northern slopes; whilst the Vara and the Magra furrow the opposite declivities. The cols and most important passages of this second division are the Buffaloro, Cento Croci, Fiumalbo, and the Pietra Mala. The great escarpments, the deep ravines, the large hollows, which give a peculiar character to this subdivision, form the spacious gulfs of Rapallo and Spezzia.- The third division of the Northern A. is composed o. a mountain mass, reaching from the sources of the Reno to Monte Coronaro, a knot from which the Savio runs to the N, and the Tiber to the S. It is by this mass of mountains that the northern part of the States-of-the-Church is separated from Tuscany. Its direction is SSE, and its extent about 68 geographical miles. It retreats from the Mediterranean, and consequently approaches the Adriatic. The southern versant is watered by the Sieve and the Arno, which have, in their upper course, a direction parallel with that of the ridge of the A. Towards the Mediterranean, the slope is much more rapid than towards the Po and the Adriatic. Thus the cities of Pisa, Modena, Florence, and Bologna, though equally distant from the crest of the A., have very different levels; Pisa being only 58 ft. above the sea, whilst Modena is 214, Florence 240, and Bologna 400. ft. The principal summits of this third part are the Piano and Falterona, whence originate the Sieve and Arno. The Central Apennines.] The Central A. commence at Monte Cornaro, and form, across the Statesof-the-Church, a barrier less elevated than that of the Northern A. It is not till we arrive at Monte Sibilla, beyond the sources of the Nera, and near Ascoli, alt. 7,228 ft., that high summits comparable to those of the Northern A. appear. Its general direction is from NNW to SSE. The Monte Velino, to the NNW of the Fucine lake, and which may be regarded as the central point of the A., determines its southern extremity: so that its length from Monte Cornaro to this point is 138 geographical miles. On the side towards the Mediterranean, it has two inclined planes; but towards the Adriatic the slope is continuous and direct. The entire course of the Tiber—whose sources are very elevated—is nearly rapidly to the NE. In general it runs parallel with I parallel with that of the upper angle of the chain. The streams which flow to the I. bank of the Tiber, form, consequently, the thalweg of short vallies all opening to the W, and descend in one much greater which runs from N to S. On the contrary, on the side towards the Adriatic, all the rivers run in a line almost direct towards the shore; the vallies running transversely to the chain of mountains whence they spring. The passes of this chain, going from N to S, are, at the village of Scheggia, between Mounts Corno and Cucco, near to which rises the Cantiano, an affluent of the Metauro; at Serravalle to the SE of Mount Pennino, at the spot where the valley of Chienti, an immediate affluent of the Adriatic, takes its rise; at Castelluccio, between Norcia and Arquate; finally, to the E of Antrodoco, the principal communication from Rieti to Aquila. Castelluccio is, perhaps, the highest village in the A., being 4,766 ft. above the sea. The Sub-Tuscan Apennines.] The Sub-Tuscan A. is a distinct group of mountains which covers Tuscany, and is contained between two sides of an angle formed by the longitudinal vallies of the Arno and the Tiber, which have their sources in Monte Cornaro, the connecting summit of the Northern and the Central A. The mountains which separate the Upper Tiber from the Upper Arno, form a transversal chain of 45 geographical miles from N to S; at the extremity of which, on its ridge, appears the lake of Perugia, a reservoir without any apparent issue. After surrounding that inland lake, it bends towards the W, where, at the distance of 18 geographical miles beyond Monte Pulciano to the SE, it separates into two branches,—one remounting to the N for 30 geographical miles; then at Mount Scalari, whence rises the Pisa, an affluent of the Arno, it takes a western direction to the sea, encompassing, in its course above Sienna, the sources of the Ombrone. By this double detour it forms 1st, the western boundary of a valley facing that of the Upper Arno; 2d, a spur, or lateral ridge, from which run all the streams which, below Florence, run into the Lower Arno. The southern branch of the Sub-Tuscan A. runs SW to Mount Amiata, where springs the Paglia, running, by the Chiana, to the Tiber. From Mount Amiata it runs SE; then passes to the N of the lake of Bolsena; and then to the E, where it undergoes a great diminution in elevation. It rises again towards Montefiascone and Viterbo, and finally terminates in a succession of low hills, which run to the sea near Civita Vecchia. This second branch forms the western boundary of the valley of the Lower Tiber, on the right of which appears the small basin of the lake of Bracciano, whose waters run to the sea near Arona. The Sub-Tuscan A. is, according to what has been above said, separated from the main range by the Tiber, the Chiano and the Arno. Between the rivers and torrents running on one side to the Arno and Tiber, and on the other to the Tuscan sea, the dividing-line establishes a series of continuous heights, having in general a direction parallel to the principal crest of the A. On the side of the Mediterranean, this particular system of mountains sends-off links, or secondary branches, reaching even to the sea-shore. That to the W detaches itself from the northern branch to the SE of Casole, joins the mountains of Montieri, and separates the basin of the Ombrone from that of the Cecina, enclosing, however, that of the Bruno, and the lake of Castiglione, near the sea. The ramification which, at Monte Amiata, detaches itself from the southern branch, goes to join Monte Sabbro, and makes towards the E the boundary of the basin of the Ombrone, augmented also on the left from that of the Albenga. The eastern versant of this ramification connects those of the S and W with the southern * APENNINES. chain of the Sub-Apennine, forming the separation of a third subdivision, in which it encounters with the particular basin of the Fiora, that of the lake of Bolsena, in which springs the Marta, a small river of the coast. The two chief branches of the Sub-Apennine Tuscan, as well as the two small branches described above, surround nearly in a semicircle the district denominated the Maremma of Sienna, which, in its lower part, is little elevated above the level of the sea, and in which the water-courses, from want of an issue, expand themselves into putrid marshes. The two principal roads across this part of the chain are those from Florence to Rome,—the eastern by Arezzo, and the other by Sienna, -which attain the two most elevated points in the two principal branches mentioned above; the first beyond Castiglioncello, and the second on this side of Radicofani. Sienna is, besides, the centre of all the secondary communications which lead to the coast, from Leghorn, from Piombino, and from Civita Vecchia. The Sub-Roman Apennines.] Near Monte Velino, before we arrive at the plateau containing the lake of Celano, the Sub-Roman A. detaches itself from the crest of the A. to the r., and towards the WSW by a principal branch 75 geographical miles in length. This branch,—which originates between the vallies of the Liris, the Salto, the Turano, and the Sacco, which run in opposite directions,—unites, by winding contours, the mountains Cantaro, Coriglio, Acuto, Carbonaro, Ceroas, and Campatri, and below the lake of Albano, loses itself in the Campagna di Roma. On its flanks it originates, in its turn, chains of mountains and small hills, of which the first range goes off from Narni on the Nera, to Sora on the Liris, and forms the valley of Velino. The second file or range embraces the valley of the Anieno, and the upper part of the Teverone; separates the course of the Liri from that of the Sacco; and near Palestrina detaches a series of small but very steep hills. This third and last branch extends between the Sacco and the Garigliano to the E, and the Pontine marshes to the W, and terminates by a steep rocky tongue of land on which is constructed the fortress of Gaeta. The Sub-Roman A. which covers the southern part of the Ecclesiastical states, is composed, therefore, of the mountains and vallies which run parallel to the main chain. It is at the foot of these latter steps that the Seven hills of Rome appear on the one side, and the Pontine marshes on the other; these last being bounded to the SW by sand-hills between which appear saline lakes communicating with the sea. Monte Circello forms the southern termination of this series of downs. In the principal branch of the Sub-Roman A., there are three passes, the 1st, between the sources of the Liri and those of the Salto, establishes the communication from Sora to Tagliacozzo, and from that point by three ramifications, to Aquila, to Rieti, and to Rome;—the 2d leads directly from Rome to Sora, passing by Zagarola and Palestrina;—the 3d, beyond the lake of Albano, is at the culminating point of the Appian way, the grand route from Rome to Naples by Terracina. Other passes also exist by the crests belonging to this spur,—as those from Rieti and Lionessa, which lead, the first to Viterbo and to Rome, the second to Spoleto and Aquila; that from Celano, on the route from Tivoli to Sulmona by Carsoli; and a lateral secondary road leading also from Carsoli by the valley of Turano. The Southern Apennines. ] This portion has the form of a fork of two branches of unequal length. The handle of this fork—the Upper Southern A.—is represented by the chain of mountains, which, from the Monte Velino, runs towards Acerenza in the Basilicata, in a NW and SE direction, at an equal distance from both seas. It is near Acerenza, and above the lake of Fesole, that the bifurcation commences. One of these branches—the inferior eastern -traverses the Terra de Bari and the Terra d'Otranto, and terminates at Cape Lucca. The other, or western inferior branch, runs across the Calabrias to the strait of Messina, terminating at the Capo Dell' Armi, formerly Leucopetra. The first of these three subdivisions—the Upper Southern A.—runs a course of 220 m. It is bristled with a great number of sharp pyramidal summits; -as Monte Forcone at the source of the Sangro; Monte St. Angelo above those of the Volturno and Trigno; Monte Chilone to the SW of Foggia; and Monte Calvello to the SE of Salerno. This southern chain has a number of passes. Among the principal communications across the range, are the pass of Sulmona, near the sources of the Volturno, so called from the city of Sulmona, where the routes to Rome, Aquila, and Pescara, unite; the road, which leads in a continuous line towards the S, by Pettorano, cuts the Upper Sangro, beyond which it passes alongst Monte Janipro, from thence descends alongst the Isernia, and afterwards forks,-one road going S to Capua, the other bending to the E by Campobasso, to Lucera and Foggia. The passes above Castel-Franco and Ariano, form the culminating points of the route from Benevento to Troja, and from Avelino to Foggia. The Sub-Vesuvian Apennines.] At an equal distance from the extreme points of the Upper Southern A., and near to these, appears the Sub-Vesuvian A. Whilst on the side towards the Adriatic, the rivers and vallies run in a direction perpendicular to that of the A.; on the opposite, they follow a direction parallel to the main range, before descending to the plains which border the Mediterranean. There are two vallies of this kind, in which originate the Sabbato and the Volturno. These two streams flow towards each other; and after their junction, run in a transversal direction to the sea. There is, therefore, an elevated terrace determined by a ridge or secondary step cut by the Volturno. To the N this ridge descends from the A., and separates the valley of the Garigliano from that of the Volturno. At Teano it subdivides; one branch extending towards Cajasso to the E of Capua; the other running W towards the Mediterranean. To the S this same ridge runs off from Monte Calvello, where the Ofanto and the Sele originate; and forms the mountains San Donato, Cervino, and Agnone. At this last summit it remounts towards the NW, forming the western limit of the basin of the Sabbato; and beyond Monte Vergino it extends towards Caserta to the l. bank of the Volturno. The direct prolongation of the spur originating beyond Monte San Donato, and passing by that of Agnone, composes a secondary series of transversal mountains, at the foot of which appears Sorrento; it is terminated by the point of Campanella, opposite the isle of Capri. From this ridge of mountains, to that which descends from Teano to the sea, extend plains and volcanic hillocks. This third Sub-A. borrows the name of Vesuvian from two mountains united by a common base, but which offer no other indication of connection with the surrounding mountains. The one of these is Somma; and the other Vesuvius, properly so called. The only passage which the Sub-Vesuvian A. presents, is that of Forchia, or the Caudian Forks, near mount Sarchio, to the NE of Naples, and on the route to Benevento. General remarks. It is from Monte Chilone that the great spur of the promontory of Il Monte Gargano runs off. This ridge, which runs NNE by E, has an extent of 60 geographical miles. It is remarkable on account of its termination, to the N of Manfredonia, in a mountain whose summit is 5,300 ft. above the sea; and because it forms what is com- APENNINES. monly called 'the spur of the boot' in this part of the Italian peninsula. The Candelaro is the only stream which, on this side of the A., has its whole course parallel with that of the main range. It originates in the elbow to the S of that spur. The oriental and lower ridge of the Southern A. begins at the lake of Pesola, which it encloses to the W and N. It runs E and N from that for an extent of more than 80 geographical miles; and, in Monte Albano, nearly reaches the shore of the Adriatic. At its commencement it is only 6 m. from the coast. Its direction deviates to the SE; towards its extremity, determined by Cape Lucca, it is wholly S. It faces the Adriatic towards the E, and the gulf of Tarentum towards the S. The principal passages in this second division of the Southern A. are along the route from Tarentum to Canosa, near the Ofanto, and along the routes of communication from Canosa and Bari to Ostuni, Mesagna, and Lecco. These routes, especially the first, frequently contract into the form of defiles. From the lake of Pesola to the Capo Dell' Armi, the length of the third and latter western part of the Southern A., is 288 m. in a line almost due S. It is to the S of Cosenza, or above the sources of the Crati, that the chain turns E; but after this deflection of 18 m., it resumes its course to the W to enclose the Upper Savuto. From the summit of the salient angle determined by that deflection, a spur, in the form of a long arch, is projected, terminating at Cape Rizzuto, the most easterly point of the gulf of Squillaci. In this part of the range, before we reach the r. bank of the Sayuto, the Southern A. approaches the sea of Sicily much nearer than the Ionian sea; beyond that torrent it is constantly at an equal distance from both. The particular branches in this direction are: that which originates between the sources of the Neto and Crati, and limits to the E the basin of the latter; those which determine on the one side to the W, the extreme parts of the gulfs of St. Euphemia, of Gioja, and of the strait of Messina; and on the other side, to the E, the most southernly point of the gulf of Squillaci; and that which forms the eastern extremity of Lower Italy, Cape Spartivento. The most practicable routes across this latter range are in the Calabrias, on the route from Naples to Sicily; and in the central provinces of the kingdom of Naples, on the route from Naples to Otranto, passing by Taranto. Geology.] The predominating rock of the A. is a white compact limestone. No primitive rocks occur in the middle part of the A., their place being taken by those of the transition class. Generally speaking, the mineralogical composition of the A., from their commencement to their termination, is one great mass of intermediary soils, to the south of which appear greywacke, and serpentine and calcareous rocks. These last furnish the greater part of the Italian marbles,—as those of Carrara, the sea-green marble of the Bocchetta, the green rocks of Florence and Prato which resemble the Verdo Antiquo, the yellow marble of Sienna, and the marble of Porto Venere on the eastern coast of the gulf of Genoa. The intermediate rocks form in some measure the centre of the chain; but there are secondary deposits of compact grey and white limestone also, of which the greater part resembles those which constitute the chain of the Jura, and under which appear deposits of gypsum which indicate the most ancient deposits. These calcareous rocks form the greater part of the lands of the Bari and Otranto. At a distance from the centre, the small hills connected with the A., on both sides of Italy, are formed of tertiary deposits, which are found both on the side of the Mediterranean and Adriatic, and which contain a great quantity of fossil shells, many of which resemble marine and the second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second second seco shells, but which bespeak an origin of much more recent date than the limestone of the central chain. Where they approach the Alps, the A. are composed of a mixture of primary rocks, viz., mica-slate, claywhich here and there repose some secondary deposits. The country on the two sides of the range preside, there is an extensive tract, chiefly along the shore, of primary and transition rocks, with only occasional patches of secondary strata. On this side also, all the volcanic rocks are found. The volcanic district of Italy is confined to that part of the peninsula which lies between the Capo Campanella, on the S side of the gulf of Naples-of which the island of Capri is a prolongation—and the mouth of the Umbro or Ombrone, a little to the S of the island of Elba,—a distance in a direct line of 230 m. The greatest breadth of this volcanic district is at Radicofani, SW of Clusium, which is 40 m. from the shore. The volcanic rocks are independent of the general geological constitution, and form, at the foot of the central chain, separate groups, which are most numerous on the western side,—as those of Santa Fiora and Viterbo; that of Latium; then those of Santa Agatha and Roccamonfina, towards Sessa; finally that of Naples. On the E side none are found but that of Mount Vultura, in Apulia, south of Melfi. Volcanic action has, however, long ceased in every part of the district except at its southern extremity. There is no active volcano but that of Vesuvius; and it is the only one on the European continent that always burns. The volcanic phenomena must not be confounded with those discharges of mud called salzes, which are frequent in the chain of Modena. In some other places gas only is disengaged, or hydrogen, —as at Villega, Pietra Mala, Barigazza, and other places in the duchy of Tuscany, of Parma, and the delegation of Bologna.—The vegetation of the A. resembles that of the Alps. To the height of 3,000 ft. they are generally clothed with evergreen oaks, chestnuts, and other forest-trees. The higher parts of the principal range are formed of bare rocks, which are covered with snow from October to May. General aspect.] The aspect of the A. is generally far from attractive: destitute of glaciers from their small elevation, and their flanks and summits equally deprived of vegetation, they appear bare and meagre. They do not present the sharp broken outlines, the pinnacles and needles, of the Alps; their summits have in general a rounded form, and their sides a more gentle slope. The highest trees met with are pines, oaks of different kinds common to the south of Europe, and beeches. The sombre verdure of the greatest part of the vegetation, (for many kinds of oaks are evergreens,) is by no means adapted to relieve the dreary perspective of these mountains. There are none of those vallies which, narrow at their commencement, gradually enlarge, and are clothed with the most varied verdure: the close contracted vallies of the A. rather resemble great ravines of a wild and rugged aspect. It is not till we approach the plains, graced with an advantageous exposure, and the genial influence of a southern climate, that we begin to see hills of olives, walnuts, cypresses, strawberries, laurels, and finally, oranges and lemons, and, as we advance farther south, palms and other trees, whose varied tints of diversified foliage refresh the eyes, wearied with the grisly aspect of the Apennines. APENRADE, a port of Denmark, at the bottom of the Apenrader-föhrde, in the Little Belt, 28 m. NNW of Sleswig; in N lat. 55° 2', E long. 9° 27'. Pop. 3,800. The bay is surrounded with hills of considerable height, covered with wood. The bailiwick of A. has an area of 135 sq. m.; and a pop. of 6,600.

APFELSTADT, a river of Coburg-Gotha, falling into the Gera at Molsdorf. Large quantities of wood are floated down this river from the district of Gotha, to that of Erfurt.—There is a village of the same name on the banks of the river. Pop. 700.
APHIBS, or KARA KUBAN, a considerable river of the Western Caucasus, rising in the great Caucasian range, and, after receiving several tributaries, flowing into the Kuban 8 m. above the Jamansu.
APHIUM. See AFIUM.
APIACA, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Matto Grosso, flowing into the Jaruenna, on the r. side.
APICE, a town of Naples, 7 m. ESE of Benevento. Pop. 2,184.
APIGNANO, a town in the States-of-the-Church, delegation of Ascoli. Pop. 1,000.
APINAC, a commune of France, in the dep. of La Loire, cant. of Saint-Bonnet. Pop. 1,120.
APIOCHAMA, a large river of La Plata, which runs to the N of La Paz, and joins the Beni, after a course from SW to NE of 70 m.
APIOKAKUMISH LAKE, a lake of Labrador, from which the Great Whale river flows towards Hudson's bay.
APLERBECK, a village of Westphalia, 24 m. NW of Arensberg. Pop. 561.
APLOGA, a market-town of Whidah, on the Slave coast of Africa.
APO, one of the Philippine islands, between Mindanao and the Calamianos, in N lat. 12° 39'.

APO, a town of Bolivia, between Arequipa and Pati. It is 7 leagues W of Pati, and only 49 ft. lower: the intervening country being a nearly level plain, covered by a thick stratum of white clay, and entirely destitute of vegetation.—Pentland.

APOLABAMBA, a prov. of Bolivia, extending 240 m. from SW to NE; bounded on the W by Carabaya, and on the E by Moxos. It is generally mountainous, but rice, cotton, and cacao, &c., are produced, which the inhabitants dispose of at La Paz.

APOLDA, a town of Saxe-Weimar-Eisenach, 8 m. N of Jena, and 40 SW of Leipsic. Pop. 3,400. The duke of Saxe-Weimar gave the town and district of A. to the university of Jena, in 1633. The town was nearly destroyed by fire in 1780. Its manufactures are woollens, kerseymeres, and stockings.
APOLIMA, an islet in the Samoan group, between Manono and Savaii. It rises to the height of 472 ft., and is evidently the crater of an extinct volcano. Pop. 75.—Wilkes.

APOLLONIA, a kingdom on the Gold coast of Africa, embracing the whole of that coast W of the river Ancobra. The exports are gold, ivory, pepper, palm-oil, and rice, which are exchanged for gunpowder, tobacco, lead, iron, fire-arms, and Indian and British cotton-goods. Its king is said to be regarded as very rich and powerful by his brother-potentates. -Cape A. is in Slat. 4° 59', W long. 2° 35'.-A. fort, to the E of the cape, is about 30 m. from Asinee.

APOLLONIA, or Suza Hammam, a ruined town on the N coast of Africa, in Cyrenaica, on a long narrow strip of elevated ground, at the bottom of a bay formed by Ras-el-Hilal and Ras Sem. There are considerable remains of its ancient magnificence. The sea appears to be constantly encroaching on the site of the ancient town.—Beechey.
APOPARO. See AMAZON, under section BENI.
APOROMA, a town of Carabaya, in Peru; it is celebrated for a gold-mine of fine quality.
APOS, a small island on the SW coast of Palawan; in N lat. 8° 5', E long. 117° 27'.
APOSTOLA THADEIA, a cape of Siberia, on the W extremity of the gulf of Anadyr, in N lat. 63°, E long. 178° 14'.
APOSTOLES, a cluster of rocks in the straits of Magalhaen, near Cape Deseado.—Also a river of Patagonia, in S lat. 49°.—Also an Indian settlement in Buenos Ayres, in S lat. 27° 54'.
APPALACHIAN MOUNTAINS. See ALLEGHANY.
APPANNEE, a river of Upper Canada, running N into Lake Ontario.
APPELHULSEN, a village of Prussia, in the gov. of Munster. Pop. 390.
APPENAN, a populous town of the island of Lombok, on a bay of the same name, near Mataran. The rajah of the island resides here.
APPENINES. See APENNINES.
APPENRODE, or ABBENRODE, a village of Prussia, in the prov. of Saxony, 7 m. SW of Osterwich. Pop. 1,060.
APPENWEYER, a village of Baden, circle of Kinzig, 5 m. NE of Offenburg. Pop. 1,300.

APPENZELL, a canton of Switzerland, consisting of the two independent republics of Appenzell-Ausser-rhoden, or A. Exterior, and Appenzell- Inner-rhoden, or A. Interior. Both divisions are entirely enclosed within the cant. of St. Gall; and unitedly present an area of about 24 m. in length, from NE to SW; varying in breadth from 5 m. to 15 m.; and containing a total superficies of about 212 sq. m. The cant. is skirted on the E, S, and SW by steep and rugged snow-capped mountainranges of calcareous formation, ramifications of the Alpine chain, the loftiest summit of which, the Sentis, rises to the height of 7,700 ft., and forms an extensive glacier. The northern districts are bristled with detached, and, in some localities, conical elevations of comparatively inconsiderable altitude. This canton, although lying in the basin of the Rhine, contains no river of importance. The principal streams are: the Sitter, a tributary of the Thur, which takes its rise in the Alp-see, a small lake in the southern district of the cant., formed by the glacier of the Sentis, and flows towards the Thur; the Urnesch; and the Goldach, in the alluvium of which gold is sometimes found. Mineral springs are numerous; the most celebrated are those of Gontin, Weissbad, and Waldstadt.—The cant. abounds in rich pasturage, and presents extensive tracts covered with forests of fir and pine; but its agricultural produce is small. The culture of grain is confined to the N of the Sitter, potatoes are raised more generally and in greater quantities; apples, pears, cherries, and, in some districts, the vine, are successfully culti- APP vated; and lint and hemp formerly constituted important articles of produce. The wealth of the cant. mainly consists in its herds of cattle, amounting in number to 23,000 head; and the rearing of which forms the exclusive industrial occupation of the Inner rhoden. Its manufactured produce consists principally in embroidered muslin, which affords employment to 10,000 persons; gauzes, cambric-muslins, and various kinds of cotton-tissues are also manufactured. There are numerous cotton-spinningmills, bleacheries, paper-mills, chemical and dyeworks, and cotton-printing establishments. The commerce consists in the exportation of these articles of manufacture, and cattle, skins, butter, honey, and cheese, which last constitutes an important article of traffic with the neighbouring Austrian territory of Suabia, from which A. derives its principal supply of corn. Its principal imports are grain, colonial produce, fruits, wines, brandy, salt, tobacco, cotton, soap, and leather. Turf is abundant, and supplies the want of wood in some districts. Game and fish are scarce. This cant. is the most populous in proportion to its extent, and occupies the 13th rank in the Swiss confederation, into which it was received in 1513. Its total federal contingent is 972 men; and 9,200 francs. There is no public debt, and several of the other cantons are largely indebted to it on mortgages. In 1597, in consequence of the dissensions occasioned by the introduction of the Reformed religion, it was divided into the two rhoden or independent communes, of which it is now composed. In the diet, the two have a common voice, which, however, must be unanimous, that is to say, this cant. has a single vote in the federacy, and its rhodes alternately send deputies. The constitution of A. is a republican democracy. The sovereign authority is exercised by the people,—either in the landsgemeinde. or general assembly, - which is composed of all citizens above the age of 18, who have received the communion, or by their delegates. The landsgemeinde is convened once a year in Appenzell, and alternately in Trogen and Hundwyl. Non-attendance is held to be an offence, and an Appenzeller is excluded for twelve months from proceeding to recover any civil debt, if his adversary can prove that he was unnecessarily absent from the landsgemeinde. It enacts laws, administers the higher functions of the executive power, and appoints all the higher functionaries of the state. The landammann, or presidents of the two republics, who are elected in each annually, preside in the general assembly as well as in the lower assemblies. The Zweifache-landrath, or executive, is composed of ten functionaries, viz., the two landammann, or first magistrates of each rhode, and eight members who are elected by the annual parochial or communal assemblies. Besides these, there are several subordinate bodies, of which the principal, called the grosse-rath, or great council, nominate the deputies to the diet, and form the high justiciary court of the republic. APPENZELL-AUSSER-RHODEN comprises the entire extent of the N and NW districts of the canton; and is divided by the Sitter-by which it is intersected from SE to NW-into two sections, named, from their local relation to that river, Vorder-Sitter, and Hinterder-Sitter. The former is subdivided into 13, and the latter into 7 communes. The total superficies of this state is about 112 sq. m.; and the number of its inhabitants was estimated in 1734 at 34,571; in 1834 at 39,857; and in 1837 at 41,080. Its federal contingent is 772 men; and 7,720 francs. The public revenues, which are unencumbered with debt, amount to 75,000 francs. Its chief towns are Herisau and Trogen. The public seminaries, under the direction or superintendence of the government, are numerous and well-supported. The number of elementary schools, -attendance at which is obligatory upon all children above the age of six years,was 75 in 1834. There are besides the Cantonal institution, a gymnasium, elementary polytechnical schools at Trogen and Huden, a school of agriculture at Trogen, a normal school at Gais, and small public libraries in the chief towns of the state. The inhabitants of the Ausser-rhoden almost exclusively profess the reformed religion. A synodal meeting of the clergy takes place annually, under the superintendence of the chief officers of the state, at which the Doyen, the highest ecclesiastical functionary, is elected.

APPENZELL-INNER-RHODEN, the smaller of the republics, forming the SE part of the cant., comprises an area of 100 sq. m.; and had a pop. in 1837 of 9,796. It consists of 7 communes, and is divided into 5 parishes. The prevailing religion is the Roman Catholic, and the district is in the dio. of Basle. In 1834, the number of elementary schools was 17. The federal contingent is 200 men; and 1,500 francs. The annual revenues amounting to 43,000 francs, are derived principally from the saltmonopoly.

APPENZELL, the cap. of the above republic of Appenzell-Inner-rhoden, is situated on the l. bank of the Sitter, in a rich and populous valley 2,538 ft. above sea-level, 6 m. SW of Trogen, 7 m. ESE of Herisau, and about the same distance S of St. Gall, in N lat. 47° 30′, and E long. 9° 24′. It consists of about 200 houses, and contains a town-house, an arsenal, a Capuchin convent, and a nunnery. In the parish church,—an edifice of the 11th century, dedicated to St. Maurice,—are preserved the banners and flags taken in the ancient wars of independence. The commerce of the town is considerable, consisting in linen and cotton fabrics, saltpetre, sharpeningstones, and timber. There are 8 annual fairs and a weekly market. A. is the residence of the principal functionaries of the state, and the place in which the annual general assembly of the people is held. Pop. 1,450, entirely Roman Catholic. In the environs are the ruins of the formerly strong castle of Claux, built in the 10th century by the abbots of St. Gallen, whence [quasi Abbatis cella, or Abten-zelle] the cant. derives its name; and at the distance of 2 m. are the mineral wells of Weissbad, the most frequented in Switzerland.
APPIANO, a town of Lombardy, in the prov. of Milan, 6 m. SW of Como.
APPIGNANO, a town of Italy, 18 m. SSW of Ancona.

APPIN, a district of Argyleshire, in Lorn, including the parish and village of the same name, and the island of Lismore. The celebrated Glencoe, the scene of one of the most disgraceful and cold-blooded massacres that stain the pages of history, is in this district, which is about 50 m. in length, and 10 in breadth. Pop. in 1841, 4,193. Houses 783.
APPINGADAM, a village of Groningen, in Holland, on the Tivel, 3 m. from the sea. Pop. 2,800.

APPLE, a small uninhabited island in the St. Lawrence, on the S side of the river, between Basque and Green islands.—Also a river on the NW of Illinois, a tributary of the Mississippi, into which it falls 20 m. below Gallena, after a course of 45 m.

APPLEBY, two parishes, a borough, and markettown in Westmoreland, situated on the Eden, and almost surrounded by it; 270 m. NNW of London, 20 m. SE of Carlisle. Pop. of the parish of A. St. Lawrence in 1841, 1,354; of the parish of A. St. Michael, or Bongate, 1,065. The two parishes constitute the town of A., which derives its chief importance from its corn-market; and returned two Capitanata, 7 m. NE of San Severo. Pop. 3,640. APR members to parliament until disfranchised by the Reform Bill.—Also a parish in Lincolnshire, 7 m. NW of Glandford-bridge. Area 5,180 acres. Pop. 505.—Also a parish partly in Derbyshire, but chiefly in Leicestershire, 6 m. SW of Ashby-de-la-Zouch. Area 2,020 acres. Pop. 1,075.

APPLECROSS, a parish in Ross-shire, extending about 25 m. along the coast. There is a copper-mine in the northern district. Gaelic is the only language. Pop. in 1821, 2,793; in 1831, 2,892; in 1841, 2,861.

APPLEDORE, a small seaport town of Devonshire, 3 m. N from Bideford, on the coast of Barnstaple bay, at the mouths of the rivers Torridge and Taw.—Also a parish and town in Kent, 63 m. ESE of London. Area 2,760 acres. Pop. 561.
APPLEDRAM, a parish in Sussex, 2 m. SW of Chichester. Area 1,070 acres. Pop. 156.
APPLEDUK, a village of Anadolia, 7 m. from Karagurgi, near the river Burghaz, containing about 40 houses.—Keppel.
APPLEGARTH, or APPLEGIRTH, a parish in Dumfries-shire, 2 m. NNE of Lochmaben. It is intersected by the Annan and the Dryfe. Pop. in 1821, 963; in 1841, 857.
APPLESHAW, a parish in Hampshire, 4 m. from Andover. Area 850 acres. Pop. 372.
APPLESTHORPE, a parish in Nottinghamshire, in North Clay division. Area 1,040 acres. Pop. 109.
APPLETHWAITE, a township in the parish of Windermere, Westmoreland. Pop. 436.

APPLETON, a village in the county of Lancaster, 3 m. from Prescot. With Widness, it has a population of 2,029.—Also a parish in Berks. Area, with Eaton township, 2,300 acres. Pop. 496.—Also a township in Budworth p., Cheshire, 4 m. SE of Warrington. Area 3,320 acres. Pop. 1,753.—Also a township in Waldo co., Maine, U. S., 35 m. E by S of Augusta. Pop. 891.
APPLETON-ROEBUCK, a township and village in the p. of Bolton - Percy, Yorkshire, 6 m. from Tadcaster. Pop. 564.
APPLETON-LE-STREET, a parish and a township in Ryedale wapentake, N. R. of Yorkshire. Area of p. 4,780 acres. Pop. 944. Area of t. 1,140 acres. Pop. 185.
APPLETON-UPON-WISKE, a parish and village in the N. R. of Yorkshire, 5 m. from Yarm. Area 1,800 acres. Pop. 559.

APPLING, a county in the S of Georgia, U. S., on the S side of the Ockmulgee. Area 1,600 sq. m. Pop. 2,052. Its cap. is Holmesville.—Also the cap. of Columbia co., 111 m. SSE of Milledgeville, on the Great Kiokee creek.
APPOIGNY, a commune and town of France, dep. of L'Yonne, 6 m. NNW of Auxerre, near the l. bank of the Yonne. Pop. 1,705.

APPOMATOX, a river of Virginia, U. S., which, after an easterly course of 120 m., unites with James river at City-point. It is navigable for vessels of 100 tons up to Petersburg, the head of tide-water. There is a canal around the falls at Petersburg, and the river is navigable by boats 80 m. above.

APPREMONT, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Vendee, cant. of Palluan, 18 m. N of Sables d'Olonne. Pop. 1,223.—Also a commune and village in the dep. of Haute-Saone, cant. of Gray. Pop. 860.
APPRIEU, a commune of France, in the dep. of Isere, cant. of Grand-Lemps. Pop. 1,480.
APPROUAQUE, a town of French Guayana, 50 m. SSE of Cayenne, near the embouchure of a river of the same name. Pop. 1,944.
APRICENA, a town of Naples, in the prov. of
APRIGLIANO, a town of Naples, 7 m. SE of | Cosenza, in Calabria Citra. Pop. 4,500.
APRIO, a town of Romania, on the Larissa, 10 m. E of Trajanopolis. It is the see of a Greek archbishop.
APS, or ALPS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ardeche, cant. of Viviers. Pop. 1,438.
APSA, or Hapsala, a town of Romania, 18 m. SE of Adrianople.
APSLEY STRAIT, a channel between Melville island and Bathurst island, off the N coast of New Holland, varying from 4 to 1½ m. in breadth, and 46 m. in length.

APT, an arrond., cant., and town of France, in the dep. of Vaucluse, 44 m. N of Marseilles. The town is a clean and well-built place, on the l. bank of the Avon, in a valley surrounded by hills; and has been formerly of considerable consequence. Under the name Apta, it was the capital of the Vulgentes. It was successively plundered by the Lombards and Saracens, and was at last united to Provence. It has manufactures of woollen cloths, and wax-candles, and trades in wine and fruits. Pop. in 1789, 3,978; in 1836, 5,758.—The arrond., comprising the five cantons of A., Bonnieux, Cadenet, Gordes, and Pertuis, has an area of 116,144 hectares, with a pop. in 1846 of 55,420.—The cant., comprising 13 communes, had a pop. in 1831 of 16,569.

APU'LIA, or Pu'glia, that portion of the kingdom of Naples which extends along the Adriatic, comprising the three administrative provinces of Capitanata, Terra de Bari, and Otranto. The ancient Apulia did not comprise the Messapian peninsula now forming the Terra d'Otranto.

APURE, a large river of South America. It rises in the eastern chain of the Andes, near St. Christopher, and passing through the llanos of Caraccas, in a north-east direction, joins the Orinoco, of which it is one of the most important tributaries, in N lat. 7° 36', W long. 66° 36', at an alt. of only 224 ft. above the sea, though more than 500 m. distant. Its waters swarm with crocodiles, manatees, and other amphibious animals. Humboldt estimates its mean fall at 14 inches in the mile; but the current in the lower part of its course is scarcely perceptible, and a strong E gale, or the least rise in the Orinoco, causes its waters to flow back. The low country on both sides of the A., above its junction with the Orinoco, is often converted into a vast lake, in which the villages and cattle-farms appear like little islets scarcely raised 2 or 3 feet above the water. When the river has again retired, the higher tracts of this plain are speedily covered with a rich sward affording excellent pasturage for several months. As the dry season advances, the grass attains a height of from 5 to 8 feet, and, becoming too coarse for food, is fired to enable the young grass to succeed it after the next rains. The congress of Venezuela have conceded to the Orinoco steam-boat company the exclusive privilege of navigating the A.
APURIMAC. See AMAZON.
AQUA, this word frequently occurs as a prefix under the forms likewise of Acqua or Agua.

AQUAMBOE, one of the petty kingdoms on the coast of Guinea, bounded on the W by the Rio Volta, and on the E by the Kerrapa. It is 20 m. broad, and 200 m. in length from E to W. The country is fertile. Salt and gold are exported from it.

AQUAPIM, a kingdom in the interior of the Gold Coast district of Africa, to the E of the Fantee country. Its mountains are covered with wood, and its vallies are fertile. It is said that the richness of its soil and beauty of its scenery are equal to any other part of the world. Maize and coffee are cultivated by the natives, who give oil and gold-dust in exchange for British manufactures. and the superior and the superior and the superior and the superior and the superior and the superior and the superior and the superior and the superior and the superior and the superior and the superior and the superior a ARA
AQUASCALIENTES. See Aguas Calientes.
AQUELAON, one of the Laccadive islands, in N lat. 10° 40', E long. 73° 56'.
AQUIAVILCO, a river of Vera Cruz, falling into the gulf of Mexico, in N lat. 18° 30'.

A'QUILA, a city of Naples, the cap. of Abruzzo Ultra Prima, and of a district of the same name. It is seated on a hill, on the banks of the Aterno or Pescara, near its source, at an alt. of 2,397 ft. above sea-level; 57 m. NE of Rome, and 120 NNW of Naples. Pop. 8,000. It is a bishop's see, immediately under the pope. The walls are about 3 m. in extent; but a great part of the enclosed space is occupied by gardens. Its manufactures consist of paper, leather, woollens, linens, and sweetmeats. Saffron is extensively raised in the environs. In 1703 it suffered severely by an earthquake. It capitulated to the Austrians in 1821. A. is about 6 m. to the S of the site of the Amiternum of the ancients, the birth-place of Sallust the historian.

AQUILEIA, an ancient but decayed city of Illyria, on the r. bank of the Isonzo, 20 m. S of Friuli. Pop. 1,500.—It was one of the largest and strongest cities in the Roman empire, and used to be designated the Second Rome, till its overthrow by Attila, in 452, when the inhabitants fled to the islands on which Venice was afterwards built.
AQUILIN (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of La Dordogne, cant. of Neuvic. Pop. 1,020.
AQUIN, a town of St. Domingo, 46 m. W of Jacquemel, on the S coast of the island.
AQUINO, a town of Naples, in the Terra di Lavora, 30 m. NW of Capua. Pop. 700. It was the birth-place of the poet Juvenal, and—as supposed by some—of Thomas Aquinas.
AQUIRE, a river of Guayana, which enters the Orinoco near its mouth, after a course of 100 m. from WSW to ENE. It is only navigable for canoes.
AQUISMON, a small town of Mexico, in the district of Valles.
